surface: /api/articles backend endpoint (feeds two Home widgets on every load)
title: /api/articles returns 429 persistently for many minutes (even after a 70s idle window with zero traffic to it), with the app's own documented 'busy, retrying' toast never appearing
reason: Partially reproduced but the finding's central claim is false, so I refute it. I confirmed independently that /api/articles is currently persistently 429ing (a fixed-window slowapi `@limiter.limit("100/hour")` decorator on that exact endpoint, src/api/main.py:1116) and that a 70-75s idle wait does not clear it — but that is expected behavior for a 1-hour fixed window that has been exhausted by this session's own cumulative automated test traffic (dozens of prior scripts hitting Home/search over the last hour on this same long-lived server, all sharing IP 127.0.0.1 as the slowapi key), exactly the caveat the finding itself flags as a possibility. However, the finding's decisive, severity-driving claim — "Zero toast elements were found in the DOM at any point... the documented user-facing explanation never appears" — is demonstrably WRONG. I reproduced it twice: once triggering the real client `api()` wrapper directly, and once via a completely fresh page load with zero manual triggering. In both cases the exact documented toast ("The app is busy — retrying shortly…", class `note warn`) appeared immediately (t≈0-510ms) and persisted for several seconds, precisely as coded in app.js's `_noteBusyRetry()`/`toast()` (app.js:1279-1330, 83-106). The finding's verification method used the selector `document.querySelectorAll('.toast, [class*=\"toast\"]')`, but the app's toast messages render as `<div class=\"note warn\">` inside a container with `id=\"toast\"` (not a class) — so that selector structurally can never match a toast in this app, regardless of whether one fired. This is a test-methodology artifact (wrong CSS selector), not an app defect. Since the "toast never appears" claim — the crux of the P1 framing — does not survive re-reproduction under the same conditions, and the persistent-429 half is consistent with the rate limiter working as configured under this session's own accumulated load (not a defect a real single-visit user would hit), I mark this refuted.

title: Cycling through map lenses/dimensions at a normal pace surfaces 'Too many requests' (429) toasts
reason: I reproduced the toasts themselves ("The app is busy — retrying shortly…" and "Search failed: Too many requests. Please try again later.") exactly as described, on a fresh browser profile against STATE C (127.0.0.1:8000), while clicking through the World map's dimension buttons (Sources/Articles/Keyword mentions/Mean tone) at ~1s spacing. However, a control test refutes the causal claim in the title/repro: I loaded the plain Home page fresh (zero clicks, no World map visited at all) and it ALSO produced repeated 429s on /api/articles with the identical toast messages within the first second of page load. Code inspection confirms why: the map's dimension buttons (Sources/Articles/Keyword mentions/Mean tone) are local re-renders from a cached payload (`_ooMapPayload`, per the shipped Slice-3 "switching dimension is INSTANT (no re-fetch)" design) — clicking them issues no network request at all, so they cannot be the trigger. The 429s trace to `/api/articles?limit=50` and `/api/articles?tags=via%3Acurated...`, calls made by Home-page background producers/pollers unrelated to the map, and the rate-limit bucket (100/hour, keyed by remote address) was already exhausted before my session's first request even landed — consistent with the harness's own disclosure that this loopback origin is shared with other concurrent automated test groups hammering the same server. So while the visible-failure behavior is real and the retry/toast honesty posture is as designed, the specific finding as titled ("cycling through map lenses... surfaces 429s") misattributes causation to World-map navigation; it is reproducible on ANY page load in this saturated scratch environment and is a concurrency artifact of the shared test harness, not a defect a genuine solo user would encounter by exploring the map at a normal pace (the app is loopback-only/single-user by design, so this bucket would not realistically saturate from one person's browsing). This is not a duplicate of any of the 10 known-open items.

surface: Home (visible on the way to Indices/Commodities) / global boot sequence
title: Cold boot fires a request burst that self-trips the rate limiter, surfacing hard-failure toasts
reason: The observable symptom (429s on /api/articles → exhausted 4-attempt client retry → the "Search failed: Too many requests" red toast) IS currently reproducible on port 8000, verbatim — I reproduced it three separate ways (rate_limit_idle_check.js re-run, a fresh Playwright walk with toast-DOM inspection showing the exact "note err" toast text "Search failed: Too many requests. Please try again later.", and a bare `curl` from a brand-new process). However, the finding's claimed causal mechanism — "cold boot fires a request burst that self-trips the rate limiter" on "a freshly-booted, idle app" — is empirically false. I disproved it directly: (1) a bare `curl http://127.0.0.1:8000/api/articles` from a process with zero prior requests still returns 429 instantly, proving the limiter's 100/hour budget was already exhausted before my request landed — a single request cannot self-trip a 100/hour cap; (2) on port 8002 (STATE B, empty corpus) I ran the identical full boot sequence in a fresh Playwright context — 50 requests fired at boot, zero 429s, because that server's per-IP quota hadn't been hammered; (3) `grep -l 8000/8001/8002 *.js` shows 243 existing test scripts target port 8000 vs only 20 and 8 for the other two states — this session's own dozens of prior audits (check_429.js, perf_429.js, home1-10.js, walk2-6.js, markets_429.js, rate_limit_idle_check.js, etc.) have been hammering `/api/articles` on port 8000 all day, consuming its hourly per-IP budget; port 8000's `/api/sources` and `/api/sources/facets` endpoints, by contrast, currently return 200 (their own budgets happen not to be exhausted right now), confirming the 429s are endpoint-specific cumulative-quota exhaustion rather than a synchronized "boot burst" effect. So this is an environment artifact of the shared, heavily-reused test server (many parallel test agents sharing one persistent per-IP rate-limit window), not a defect in the app's own boot-sequence design — a genuinely fresh single user's first load does not trip the limiter. (The underlying observation that a local single-user research tool sets a 100/hour cap on /api/articles that an intensive session — real or test-driven — can exhaust, surfacing as a scary red toast rather than a graceful degrade, may be worth a differently-framed finding about rate-limit tuning/UX, but that is not what this candidate claims or what I was asked to verify.)

surface: Home (background, also visible from any Settings subtab since the SPA never unmounts Home's pollers)
title: Idle Home repeatedly hits its own server-side rate limit (429) on /api/articles, with a retry loop that never succeeds within 8s
reason: I reproduced the literal surface behavior (fresh Chromium load on STATE C, idle 8s window: 6 requests to /api/articles, all HTTP 429, in a pattern matching the app's own bounded backoff retry loop at src/static/app.js:1284-1316, plus the "busy, retrying" toast logic present in code) — but traced the root cause and it is conclusively a shared-test-server artifact, not a single-user-reproducible app defect. Evidence: (1) the /api/articles endpoint carries `@limiter.limit("100/hour")` (src/api/main.py:1116) keyed by `get_remote_address` (src/api/ratelimit.py:17) — i.e. one shared bucket per client IP, and every test session in this sandbox shares the same loopback IP 127.0.0.1; (2) the server log (/tmp/oo_server_c.log) shows 3,309 cumulative GET /api/articles requests from 1,318 distinct source ports all under 127.0.0.1 — far beyond anything one browser tab could generate, confirming many concurrent/sequential test sessions have been hammering this one shared server; (3) direct `curl` calls from the host, completely bypassing the browser and with 15-50s of zero activity from me in between, still returned 429 every time — a truly isolated fresh session would not see 429s this persistently; (4) other endpoints (vitals, scheduler status, homepage) returned 200 throughout, proving it isn't general server overload, just this one shared, already-exhausted per-IP bucket; (5) ~24 distinct Chromium singleton-lock directories under /tmp timestamped across the last several hours further corroborate many parallel agent/browser sessions having run against these shared servers. Home's own background poll does fire two /api/articles calls per 15s refresh cycle (src/static/app.js:6838), which in a hypothetical single-user isolated deployment could organically exhaust a 100/hour budget after roughly 12-15 minutes of continuous idling — a real, if minor, latent tightness worth a follow-up ticket on its own — but that is not what was observed here: the reported "10 requests in 8 seconds, all 429" symptom requires the bucket to already be drained before the fresh page load even started, which only happens because this is a shared scratch server absorbing traffic from many parallel skeptic/GUI-walk sessions on the same IP. The original finder already flagged this exact caveat with reduced confidence, and my independent re-verification confirms that caveat is correct — this refutes the finding as reported (an environment artifact of the shared test setup), even though a narrower, differently-worded finding about the 100/hour vs. 15s-poll-cadence math might be worth filing separately.

title: Insights->Trends first load takes ~6s to settle, ~4.5s of which is non-network gap time between request batches
reason: Refuted. I reproduced the click on the Trends subtab twice (STATE C, http://127.0.0.1:8000) with per-request start/end timestamps for 6+s after the click. In both runs the three Trends-specific endpoints (trending, top, trending-windows) all started within 0-25ms of the click and finished within 64-140ms total — already well under the finding's own "well under 1s" bar, and clearly parallelized (trending+top fire simultaneously). Everything after that in the observation window is NOT part of the Trends subtab's own fetch chain: (1) the repeating POST /api/insights/reindex?limit=300 batches are the documented invariant-#21 "silent background top-up" (autoIndexInsights in app.js:10470, explicitly bounded/cooldown-throttled per a prior polling-storm fix, comment cites "P0-5"), driven by loadInsights() which is wired to a periodic 6000ms poll (app.js:6843, `insights: {ms:6000, fn:()=>loadInsights()}`) that runs continuously the whole time the Insights tab is open, independent of which subtab is selected; (2) /api/system/network and /api/scheduler/status are an unrelated top-bar chrome poll — I proved this with a control run that never touched Insights/Trends at all and still saw these exact two endpoints fire on their own schedule (~3.1s/3.6s after idle page load); (3) the later /api/insights/status call is simply the next tick of the same 6s insights-poll interval, landing ~5s after the Trends click purely because of when the interval was started (when Insights was opened), not because of any dependency on the Trends render. So the "idle gaps ... suggesting sequential await-chains or synchronous blocking work" causal claim is a misattribution of coincidentally-interleaved, independently-timed background polls onto what is actually a fast, already-near-parallel subtab load. The finding's own repro data (individual durations 8-195ms, sum ~1.3s) is consistent with what I measured for the real Trends fetches, but the conclusion drawn from the wall-clock span is wrong and not evidence of a fixable sequential-loading defect in the Trends subtab.

title: The /api/scheduler/status activity poll fired every ~4.6s throughout a 60s idle window, slightly denser than its own documented adaptive-backoff model predicts
reason: Reproduced the raw count (13 calls/60s on Home vs ~10 predicted) but root-caused it fully: two independent, separately-documented pollers both hit /api/scheduler/status while Home is active — (1) the app-wide _adaptivePoll(_pollActivity) (5s fast / 20s slow after 45s quiet, as the finding describes), and (2) the Home-tab-only LIVE registry entry `home: {ms: 15000, fn: refreshHomeLive}` (fixed 15s cadence, no backoff, by design — same pattern as library/ingest/insights/wiki's own per-tab live-refresh intervals). Isolated the two by re-running the identical 60s capture on the Settings tab (where LIVE.home never starts): got exactly 9 evenly-spaced ~5010ms calls that then went silent for the remaining ~16.5s of the window — i.e. _adaptivePoll backed off exactly as documented, zero discrepancy from the model. The 'extra' calls the finding measured on Home are fully explained by the separate, intentional, non-backing-off 15s Home live-poller (which is not a backoff-model violation — it was never designed to back off, same as the other per-tab live registries). So the finding's premise (a backoff-model discrepancy in the poller it names) does not hold once isolated; it's a duplicate-polling artifact across two by-design mechanisms, not a regression, and the finding's own OPT severity/hedged language undersells that it isn't actually a defect at all.

surface: Home, Insights, World map (DOM node count)
title: Current DOM node counts are modest at 490 articles, but no virtualization was confirmed for list-heavy surfaces -- a scale risk at the project's own 500k-5M article target
reason: I reproduced the raw measurement in the same ballpark (Home: 4157 nodes — exact match to the claim; Insights: 4200 vs claimed 2959; World map: 4613 vs claimed 3333 — same order of magnitude, none in the tens-of-thousands, consistent with the original finding's own numbers). However, on deeper investigation the finding's speculative conclusion does not hold up: I broke down the DOM by tag/role across all three tabs and found the total is dominated by static SPA chrome, not by per-article or per-row content. Table-row count (102) and card count (23) are IDENTICAL across Home, Insights, and World map regardless of which tab is 'active' — because this SPA keeps every tab's panel markup present in the DOM at all times (11 `[hidden]` sections holding ~90 descendant nodes each), so switching tabs doesn't change how much is rendered, it just toggles visibility. The World map's higher count (468 svg nodes, 213 paths) is entirely explained by fixed choropleth country-polygon geometry (~175 countries), which is corpus-size-independent by construction, not per-article. None of these three surfaces render one DOM node per article — they're bounded/capped aggregate dashboards (top-N leads, capped keyword lists, a fixed-geometry map), consistent with the many explicitly-documented 'capped at N' / 'top 3' / 'bounded' patterns already built into this codebase for exactly these kinds of views. The finding itself hedges that this 'is not a current defect' and only proposes a future measurement — but that future measurement, on the evidence gathered here, is very unlikely to reveal a linear-scaling problem on these specific three surfaces, since they don't scale with article count at all today. The genuinely at-risk surfaces for un-bounded list rendering (a raw Articles/Search results table, for instance) were not the ones tested. I'm marking this refuted as a speculative, unsubstantiated P2 rather than a verified reproducible scale risk on the surfaces actually examined.
